steps.instruction: Here we have a superpower we never get in real research: we **know the true population mean is exactly μ = 50** (see the population line above the controls). That lets us check whether each confidence interval actually captures the truth. A blue box labeled **"Predict first"** is sitting above the chart — type a guess for how many of the next **25** intervals will *miss* μ, then click **Lock & draw 25**.

gate.question: Before you draw — at 95% confidence, how many of 25 intervals do you expect to MISS μ = 50?

choices.feedback: Good instinct — lock it in and see. 5% of 25 is about 1.25, so 0–2 misses is typical.
Lock in your guess and watch. (95% confidence is a high bar — misses should be rare, not half.)
Lock it in and see — at 95% confidence some intervals SHOULD miss, just not many.

steps.observe: After you click **Lock & draw 25**, the tool tells you how many of your 25 actually missed and compares it to your guess.

steps.instruction: Look at the chart. Most intervals are drawn one color (they **capture** μ — the vertical line at 50 passes through them); a few are highlighted because they **miss** (the line at 50 falls outside them).
steps.observe: Count the misses — usually about **1 of 25**. Each interval is one study's attempt to pin down μ from a sample of 25.

gate.question: What does it mean for one interval to "miss" μ?

choices.text: The sample mean x̄ was wrong / miscalculated
The interval's range does not include the true value 50
The sample was too small to count

gate.feedback: Exactly — a miss means the interval's low-to-high range simply doesn't contain 50. The method still 'worked'; this is the expected ~5% of intervals that fall short. Nothing went wrong with that sample.
gate.retryFeedback: Look at a highlighted interval and the vertical line at 50. A 'miss' is purely about whether that line lands inside or outside the box.

steps.instruction: Now click **+100** to draw many more intervals. Watch the running **coverage rate** in the results panel climb toward a stable value.
steps.observe: After a few hundred intervals, the share that capture μ settles near **95%** — about **5 in 100 miss**, no matter how many you draw.

gate.question: Pick out any single interval that MISSED. Is it correct to say "there is a 95% probability that THIS interval contains μ"?

choices.text: Yes — every 95% interval has a 95% chance of containing μ
No — that interval is already drawn; it either contains 50 or it doesn't. The 95% describes the method across many intervals, not this one.
No — it's actually a 5% probability for that interval

gate.feedback: Right. Once an interval is drawn, the truth is fixed: 50 is either inside it or not (the missed one definitely is NOT). "95%" is a property of the *procedure* — repeated on fresh samples, it captures μ about 95% of the time. It is not a probability about any single, already-computed interval.
gate.retryFeedback: The interval you're pointing at already missed — so its chance of containing 50 is 0, not 95%. What, then, does the 95% actually refer to?

steps.instruction: **Hover** (or tap) any interval. Instead of a flat bar, it shows a **peaked** shape — a cat's-eye.
steps.observe: The bar bulges in the middle (near x̄) and tapers at the ends.

gate.question: What is the cat's-eye telling you about the values inside a confidence interval?

choices.text: Every value in the interval is equally plausible
Values near the center (x̄) are more plausible than values out near the endpoints

gate.feedback: Yes. A confidence interval is not a list of equally-likely values. Estimates near x̄ are best supported by the data; the endpoints are the least plausible values still inside the net. "In the interval" doesn't mean "all equally likely."
gate.retryFeedback: Look at where the shape is fattest versus thinnest, and what that says about which values the data support most.

steps.instruction: Change the **Confidence level** dropdown from 95% to **99%**. The intervals you've already drawn re-draw at the new level.
steps.observe: The intervals get **wider**, and the coverage rate climbs toward **99%** — now only about **1 in 100 misses**.

gate.question: Why does demanding 99% confidence make every interval wider?

choices.text: To miss less often, each interval must cast a wider net to be more sure of catching μ
Higher confidence shrinks the intervals to be more precise

gate.feedback: Right — there's an unavoidable trade-off. Higher confidence buys a higher capture rate, but only by widening the interval (less precision). The only way to get BOTH more confidence and more precision is a larger sample.
gate.retryFeedback: If you want to miss only 1% of the time instead of 5%, does your net need to be bigger or smaller?

steps.instruction: Time to put it together. In real research you get **one** sample, compute **one** interval — and you never get to draw the vertical truth line, because you don't know μ.
steps.reveal: So when a study reports a 95% confidence interval, "95% confident" does **not** mean there's a 95% chance the true value sits in that one interval — it already does or it doesn't. It means the interval came from a **procedure that captures the truth about 95% of the time**. You trust this particular interval the way you'd trust a method that's right 19 times out of 20. That's the whole idea: we can't certify any single interval, but we can rely on a method with a known, high success rate.
